# Unofficial Meeting Notes
## Conroe ISD Audit Committee Meeting
### May 13, 2020 — Regular Meeting

> **Note:** These are unofficial notes based on an auto-generated transcript and may contain errors or omissions.

---

## Attendees
- **Committee Chair:** Mr. Sanders
- **Committee Members/Participants:** Mr. Hayden (Internal Audit), Mr. Rice (Finance), Mr. Moore, Mr. Williams, Miss Blacklock, and others
- **Administration:** Dr. Knoll (Superintendent), Darren (likely Darren from Finance), Mr. Foster (Planning & Construction), Dr. Hahn (joined via call)

---

## 1. Citizens Participation
No citizens signed up to speak.

---

## 2. Approval of Prior Meeting Minutes (January 8, 2020)
Minutes from the January 8 meeting were distributed via email.

**Vote:** Motion to approve as presented — **Passed unanimously (voice vote, all ayes)**

---

## 3. EthicsPoint Reports
Mr. Hayden presented the EthicsPoint report:
- **Three reports received; all three are closed.**
- No questions from committee members.

---

## 4. 2019-2020 Internal Audit Plan — Changes

Mr. Hayden presented the following changes to the audit plan:

- **Payroll audit moved to Fall 2020.**
- **Two construction audits added** in its place, with assistance from **Weaver** (external firm). The goal is to build institutional knowledge within Internal Audit so future construction audits can be performed in-house, saving money long-term.
- **All Activity Fund Audits for the year are completed.**
- **Follow-up audits reduced from five to three** to redirect resources toward the higher-value construction audits.

**Vote:** Motion to accept the EthicsPoint report and approve the audit plan changes — **Passed unanimously (voice vote, all ayes)**

---

## 5. Activity Fund Audits — Summary
Mr. Hayden confirmed all Activity Fund Audits for the year were completed with a good sampling of schools across the district. Going forward, the focus will shift toward **high-value, high-risk items** rather than Activity Fund audits.

---

## 6. Consulting Engagement: Construction Manager at Risk (CMAR) vs. Competitive Sealed Proposal (CSP)

### Internal Audit Recommendation:
- Conroe ISD currently uses **only the CMAR method** for construction.
- **All peer districts use CSP**, and of the 20 districts represented by Mickey Morris, Conroe ISD is **the only one that does not use CSP**.
- Internal Audit recommends the **full board approve using the CSP method** and designate **one of the four new elementary schools in the bond as a CSP pilot project**.
- Potential savings of **up to 5%** were cited — on a $30 million project, that could mean **$1.5 million in savings**.

### Discussion and Concerns:

**Mr. Foster (Planning & Construction)** was brought into the call to provide input:
- **CSP** is essentially a lump-sum bid — contractor gives a fixed price; changes require change orders.
- **CMAR** is more of a contractor partnership — allows less detailed drawings with flexibility to achieve desired outcomes without as many change orders.
- Mr. Foster expressed a **preference for CMAR**, citing his department's efficiency with the current method and concerns about CSP:
  - CSP is **more staff-intensive** during construction. His department has **5.5 staff** compared to Katy ISD's approximately **16 staff**, which uses CSP.
  - CSP could lead to **less desirable contractors** winning on price alone.
  - CMAR delivers **final cost savings** at project completion, while CSP aims for **first cost savings**.
  - He cited an example of Humble ISD bidding one elementary school **three times** under CSP before getting close to budget, and still having to increase the budget.
- Mr. Foster confirmed he **could successfully build a school under either method** and noted that CSP is the **state's default method** under government code, while the Conroe ISD board previously set CMAR as its local default.

**Committee member Del** asked whether the district can choose project-by-project — **confirmed yes**, the board can select the method on a per-project basis.

**Mr. Sanders** raised concerns about contractor performance risks under CSP, including penalties for lateness and the risk of contractors walking away from projects.

**Dr. Knoll** cautioned:
- The next elementary (Grand Oaks) **does not yet have land acquired** and may be on a short timeline.
- Recommended being **strategic about timing** for a CSP pilot — suggested the **last elementary** in the bond program may be a better candidate to avoid sabotaging the pilot.
- Emphasized this was **not the time for a full debate** since not all board members were present; it will need to be placed on a future full board agenda.

### Outcome:
**No vote taken** — this was presented as an Internal Audit recommendation/report only. Further discussion will occur at a future full board meeting.

---

## 7. PEIMS Attendance Audit

### Key Findings:
- **Not all schools were in compliance** with TEA attendance-taking requirements.
- Attendance-taking times were **highly inconsistent** — some recorded as early as **5:00 a.m.** and as late as **6:00 p.m.**

### Recommendations:
- Schools should be reminded of the **importance of compliance** due to risk of fines and impact on revenue streams.
- The **PEIMS office will conduct audits of schools four times per year**, incorporating the data-crunching methodology used by Internal Audit.

### Administration Response:
- **Dr. Knoll** made it a priority at the next principals' meeting, quantifying the dollar amount at risk from attendance errors.
- **Dr. Hahn** confirmed training was conducted at principals' meetings twice, reviewing standard practices, and working directly with specific campuses where problems were identified.
- Dr. Knoll shared an example of **Grand Oaks High School** implementing a structured process where a student announcement reminds teachers to take attendance at the designated time.

---

## 8. Audit Report Format
Mr. Hayden asked the committee for feedback on the report format being used, with a focus on being **more data-driven** (quantifying costs and risks).

**Committee consensus:** The format works well. Members appreciated having the full report in advance and the summary presentation during the meeting.

---

## 9. Campus Audits During Principal Transitions

A committee member asked whether **cursory audits during principal changes** would continue, given the audit schedule changes.

- **Mr. Hayden:** Currently not planned due to limited resources being redirected to higher-risk areas, but could be added back if the committee desires.
- **Dr. Knoll** proposed that the **finance office accountant** who coordinates Activity Funds could perform **high-level audits during principal/secretary transitions** and flag concerns to Internal Audit.
- Mr. Hayden supported this as a **good internal control** — audit can then review the control process rather than visiting each campus.
- **Darren Rice** noted that reconciling accounts at transition points could involve some historical review, but agreed a robust audit process wasn't needed for routine transitions.

---

## 10. Procurement Audit — Status Update
- Currently in progress and **winding down**.
- Final report expected to be delivered to the committee **by the end of May 2020**.

---

## 11. Next Meeting Date
- **Tentative date set: Wednesday, September 9, 2020** (ahead of the September 15 board meeting).
- Acknowledged uncertainty about what September may look like due to COVID-19; date will be firmed up over the summer as TEA provides more clarity.

---

## 12. Adjournment

**Vote:** Motion to adjourn — **Passed unanimously (voice vote)**

---

| Next meeting | Tentatively **September 9, 2020** |
